title: Assign AI governance ownership before deployment
text: AI deployment should have named owners, escalation paths, documented policies, and review routines so risks are managed as part of normal organizational governance.

title: Map context, stakeholders, and intended use
text: Before AI use expands, teams should identify the intended workflow, affected stakeholders, known limitations, and potential impacts in that specific context.

title: Measure AI risks before scaling
text: AI risks should be assessed with observable tests, metrics, and review evidence before moving from a pilot into wider operational use.

title: Use risk controls and stop conditions
text: Risk management should include controls, monitoring, escalation, and stop conditions when AI behavior creates unacceptable error, safety, privacy, or trust risk.

title: Review generated content before use
text: Generative AI outputs can be inaccurate, fabricated, or inappropriate; user workflows should include review before outputs affect customers, records, or decisions.

title: Protect sensitive data in GenAI workflows
text: Generative AI workflows should avoid unnecessary entry of sensitive, confidential, or personal data and should preserve approved data-handling boundaries.

title: Disclose AI involvement when trust depends on it
text: When AI use changes expectations for authorship, review, or accountability, teams should disclose the role of AI and who remains responsible for the output.

title: Minimize personal data collection and use
text: AI adoption should limit personal data collection to what is needed for the workflow and should avoid secondary use that users would not reasonably expect.

title: Treat AI tools as part of the security environment
text: AI tools used in workflow adoption should be governed with access control, approved systems, incident paths, and third-party security review where relevant.

title: Avoid automated high-stakes decisions without human review
text: AI systems should not automate high-stakes decisions in areas such as education, employment, finance, legal, medical, housing, or essential services without appropriate human review.

title: Do not use AI to compromise privacy
text: AI use should not aggregate, monitor, profile, or distribute private or sensitive information without authorization and appropriate safeguards.

title: Use special safeguards for minors
text: AI deployments involving children or teens should include heightened safeguards, age-appropriate boundaries, and protection against harmful or manipulative uses.

title: Keep teachers and institutions accountable
text: Generative AI in education should support learning and teaching while preserving human responsibility, institutional oversight, and safeguards for learners.
source: UNESCO Guidance for Generative AI in Education and Research

title: Check equity and access impacts in education AI
text: Education AI adoption should consider whether different learner groups experience unequal access, bias, or uneven quality from the system.
source: UNESCO Guidance for Generative AI in Education and Research

title: Protect education records and parent/student rights
text: Student education records and personally identifiable information require careful disclosure controls, parent or eligible-student rights, and clear handling practices.
source: FERPA parent and eligible student guides

title: Review online education service data practices
text: Schools should understand what student data an online service collects, how it is used, who receives it, and whether school control and disclosure rules are preserved.
source: Student Privacy and Online Educational Services

title: Be careful with sensitive student surveys
text: Education workflows that collect sensitive student information should provide appropriate notice, consent, and limits on how that information is gathered or used.
source: Protection of Pupil Rights Amendment model notice

title: Train staff on education data security
text: Education data security programs should include staff training, access controls, breach-aware practices, and clear responsibility for protecting student information.

title: Protect worker well-being during AI adoption
text: Workplace AI adoption should improve work quality and opportunity without using AI in ways that undermine worker well-being, autonomy, or trust.
source: U.S. Department of Labor AI guidance

title: Explain workplace AI use and worker impact
text: Workers should receive understandable information about how AI tools are used in their workflow and whether AI outputs affect evaluation, scheduling, monitoring, or access to opportunity.
source: U.S. Department of Labor AI guidance

title: Use training that supports workers, not only tool usage
text: AI enablement should include training, feedback channels, and worker participation so adoption is not reduced to usage pressure or productivity surveillance.
source: U.S. Department of Labor AI guidance

title: Do not treat HR AI as neutral by default
text: Employment-related AI tools should be evaluated for validity, disparate impact, accessibility, and compliance before outputs affect hiring, promotion, discipline, benefits, or accommodations.
source: EEOC employment tests and selection procedures

title: Preserve human review for accommodation-sensitive HR workflows
text: AI use in HR workflows involving leave, benefits, or accommodations should preserve human review and clear escalation because errors can affect protected rights and employee trust.
source: EEOC employment tests and selection procedures

title: Lawyers remain responsible for AI-assisted work
text: Legal AI use should preserve competence, review, supervision, communication duties, confidentiality, and lawyer responsibility for the final work product.

title: Avoid confidential legal data in unsafe AI contexts
text: Legal teams should verify that AI systems are approved for the data involved and should avoid exposing confidential client or matter information outside permitted boundaries.

title: Validate models before relying on forecasts
text: Model use should include validation, monitoring, documentation, and review of assumptions before model output becomes part of financial planning or accountability routines.

title: Document audit trails for model-assisted decisions
text: Model-assisted workflows should document inputs, assumptions, limitations, review evidence, and who approved use of the output.

title: Honor privacy and confidentiality commitments
text: Organizations should not use customer, employee, or confidential data for AI training or AI features in ways that contradict privacy promises or reasonable confidentiality expectations.
source: FTC AI privacy and confidentiality guidance

title: Avoid unsupported AI performance claims
text: AI claims should be accurate, evidence-backed, and not misleading about what the system can do, especially when claims affect safety, screening, or consumer decisions.

title: Do not overstate AI screening accuracy
text: AI screening or detection products can create consumer and public-safety harm when organizations overstate accuracy or fail to disclose limits and failure modes.
source: FTC Evolv Technologies AI screening action

title: Use govern-map-measure-manage release routines
text: Responsible AI programs should combine governance, risk identification, measurement, mitigation, release review, and deployment monitoring.

title: Escalate sensitive AI uses
text: AI use cases with heightened social, safety, legal, or rights impact should receive additional review and mitigation before deployment or expansion.

title: Frame AI as augmenting human intelligence
text: Enterprise AI adoption should position AI as augmenting human capability rather than replacing human judgment, especially when worker trust and professional identity are blockers.
source: IBM Principles for Trust and Transparency

title: Respect client data ownership and insight boundaries
text: Enterprise AI tools should preserve client or organizational ownership of data and derived insights, with clear boundaries for storage, processing, and secondary use.
source: IBM Principles for Trust and Transparency

title: Maintain an inventory of AI use cases
text: Organizations can improve AI governance by tracking AI use cases, status, purpose, responsible office, and risk-management information in an inventory.
source: Public AI use case inventory examples

title: Use QA review before customer-facing AI drafts
text: Customer support AI drafts should be tested on low-risk or archived tickets first, then reviewed for tone, policy accuracy, and customer harm before live use.
source: NIST GenAI Profile / FTC AI guidance

title: Keep generated code in reviewable low-risk tasks first
text: AI coding assistant adoption should begin with reversible tasks such as explanations, tests, or low-risk refactors, with human code review before generated output affects production.
source: NIST GenAI Profile / NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
